q: Many different types of ___ play an important part in our daily life.
Spectacles, cameras, microscopes and telescopes are useful ___ instruments employing lenses.
The function and formation of images in these instruments are easily understandable with the help of ___ diagrams.
In this chapter, we will study optical instruments and their ___.
A camera is an ___ instrument that forms and records an image of an object.
The image may be recorded on ___ (or) it may be detected by an electronic sensor that stores the image digitally.
Regardless of how the image is recorded, all cameras form images in the same ___ way.
A ___ focuses the light from the object to form a real inverted image on the film or electronic sensor.
A convex lens focuses the light from the object to form a ___ inverted image on the film or electronic sensor.
A convex lens focuses the light from the object to form a real ___ image on the film or electronic sensor.
A convex lens focuses the light from the object to form a real inverted image on the ___ or electronic sensor.
A ___ is placed behind the convex lens to control the amount of light energy falling on the film.
A diaphragm is placed behind the convex lens to control the amount of ___ energy falling on the film.
The ___ is a circular opening through which the light passes.
The aperture can be made wide or narrow by adjusting the ___.
The size or the diameter of the aperture is expressed in terms of ___.
The f-number of a camera is the ratio of the ___ of the lens to the diameter of aperture.
The smaller the f-number, the ___ the aperture is.
When taking a photograph in bright light a ___ f-number (narrow aperture) must be used.
When taking a photograph in dim light a ___ f-number (wide aperture) must be used.
A ___ controls the time during which light enters the camera through the lens.
The shutter speed is expressed in terms of the ___ time.
The shorter the exposure time, the ___ the shutter speed is.
A camera consists of a ___ ring by which the distance between the lens and the film can be adjusted to obtain a sharp image on the film.
The distance between the lens and the film can be adjusted to obtain a ___ image on the film using a focusing ring.
The film at the back of the camera is actually a ___ which receives the image.
The film consists of a thin layer of ___ compounds of silver.
___ reaction occurs when light energy falls on the film and as a result the image of an object is formed on the film.
In a digital camera, the film is replaced by an ___ sensor.
An electronic sensor is made up of millions of tiny ___ which response to light.
Human eye consists of ___ organs which are capable of receiving visual images.
The human eye sends visual information to the ___.
The human eye is very similar to a ___ in many aspects.
The ___ opens and closes the eye, similar to a camera's lens cover.
The front portion of the eye is covered by a tough, transparent membrane called the ___.
The cornea is a tough, ___ membrane covering the front of the eye.
The region between the cornea and the eyelens is filled with a transparent liquid called the ___ humour.
The ___ humour is a transparent liquid found between the cornea and the eyelens.
The region between the eyelens and the retina contains a transparent liquid called the ___ humour.
The ___ humour is a transparent liquid found between the eyelens and the retina.
The ___ is the colored portion of the eye.
The iris controls the amount of ___ energy entering the eye.
The ___ is in the centre of the iris and the light passes through it.
The pupil is similar to the ___ in the camera.
The ___ adjusts the size of the pupil in the eye.
The pupil automatically ___ in dim light.
The pupil automatically ___ in bright light.
The ___ muscles are attached to the eyelens.
The ciliary muscles can vary the ___ of the eyelens.
Ciliary muscles alter the ___ of the lens to obtain a sharp image on the retina.
When near objects are viewed, the ciliary muscles ___ and permit the eyelens to form a more convex shape.
Viewing near objects causes the eyelens to form a more ___ shape.
When distant objects are viewed, the ciliary muscles ___ and the eyelens becomes less convex.
Viewing distant objects causes the eyelens to become ___ convex.
The ___ is the light sensitive area of the cells, at the back of the eye.
The retina consists of ___ and cones.
The ___ spot is the most light-sensitive spot on the retina.
The yellow spot on the retina is used for viewing very ___ objects.
The yellow spot consists of ___ exclusively.
There is a spot on the retina which has no ___ or cones.
When an image is formed at the blind spot it ___ be seen at all.
The spot where the optic nerve enters the eye is called the ___ spot.
The light energy falling on the retina is transmitted to the brain by means of the ___ nerve.
The cornea, the aqueous humour, the eyelens and the vitreous humour are ___ substances.
The image formed on the retina is ___ and inverted.
The image formed on the retina is real, ___ and smaller than the size of the object.
The image formed on the retina is smaller than the ___ of the object.
The common defects of vision are ___ (hyperopia) and nearsightedness (myopia).
___ is a defect of vision in which distant objects are seen clearly, but objects nearby are blurry.
In farsightedness, distant objects are seen clearly, but objects ___ are blurry.
___ is a defect of vision in which near objects are seen clearly, but objects far away are blurry.
In nearsightedness, near objects are seen clearly, but objects ___ away are blurry.
Defects of vision can be caused by ciliary muscles that cannot contract or relax ___.
Defects of vision can also be caused by wrongly shaped ___.
For farsightedness, rays from near objects intersect at a point ___ the retina.
Farsightedness can be due to an eyeball which is too ___.
Farsightedness can be due to an eyelens which cannot be sufficiently ___.
Farsightedness can be corrected by using spectacles with a ___ lens.
Farsightedness can be corrected by using spectacles with a ___ lens.
For nearsightedness, rays from distant objects intersect at a point in ___ of the retina.
Nearsightedness can be due to an eyeball which is too ___.
Nearsightedness can be due to an eyelens which is more ___ than is necessary.
Nearsightedness can be corrected by using spectacles with a ___ lens.
Nearsightedness can be corrected by using spectacles with a ___ lens.
People with normal vision can see an object in greatest ___ at a certain distance from the eye.
The least distance of distinct vision is about ___ cm.
People with normal vision can see clearly objects which are at ___.
For normal vision the maximum distance of distinct vision is ___.
When objects at infinity are viewed, the eyes are ___.
A ___ is an optical instrument which uses lenses to make enlarged images of objects.
A microscope uses lenses to make ___ images of objects.
Microscopes are used for objects too small for the ___ eye to see.
___ optics is applied to analyse the formation of an image in the microscope.
A compound microscope has at least ___ convex lenses.
The two convex lenses in a compound microscope are an ___ lens and an eyepiece lens.
The lens nearer the object in a compound microscope is called the ___.
The lens through which the final image is viewed in a compound microscope is called the ___.
When using a compound microscope, the object should be placed between $F_o$ and $___$.
In a compound microscope, $F_o$ represents the focus of the ___.
The objective lens of a compound microscope produces an image which is ___.
The image produced by the objective lens is real, ___ and larger than the size of the object.
The image produced by the objective lens is ___ than the size of the object.
The image formed by the objective lens is between the eyepiece E and its focus ___.
The eyepiece ___ the image formed by the objective.
The final image formed by a compound microscope is ___.
The final image formed by a compound microscope is virtual and on the ___ side as the object.
The final image can be seen clearly when the eye is very close to the ___.
The magnifying power of a compound microscope is the product of the magnification of the ___ and the magnifying power of the eyepiece.
For a large magnifying power of a compound microscope, both the objective and the eyepiece should have ___ focal lengths.
The focal length of the objective should be ___ than that of the eyepiece in a compound microscope.
Compound microscopes have a magnifying power of several ___.
As the magnifying power increases, the ___ of images is reduced.
Compound microscopes are used to study ___ cells.
An ___ microscope employs electron beams in place of light.
Electron microscopes use ___ lenses.
Electron microscopes offer high magnification and ___.
Optical microscopes have a maximum magnifying power of ___.
Electron microscopes can reach a magnification of ___.
Electron microscopes deliver a more ___ image compared to optical microscopes.
Optical microscopes use ___ lenses, whereas electron microscopes use electrostatic or electromagnetic lenses.
Optical microscopes use ___ or light energy, while electron microscopes use electrons.
Electron microscopes use electrons, which have ___ wavelengths.
In an electron microscope, a beam of accelerated ___ is used as a source of illumination.
Two main types of electron microscope are the Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) and the ___ Electron Microscope (TEM).
The accelerated electron beam is focused by ___ or electromagnetic lenses.
The magnified electron image in an electron microscope can be projected onto a ___ viewing screen.
The image from an electron microscope can be recorded by a ___ film or by a digital camera.
Electron microscopes are used to investigate the ___ of a wide range of biological and inorganic specimens.
An optical instrument used for viewing distant objects is called a ___.
Two types of telescope are ___ telescope and terrestrial telescope.
A refracting astronomical telescope consists of an objective of ___ focal length and an eyepiece of short focal length.
A refracting astronomical telescope consists of an eyepiece of ___ focal length.
In a refracting astronomical telescope, both lenses are ___ lenses.
___ invented a refracting telescope.
In an astronomical telescope, a real and inverted image of a distant object is formed at the focus of the ___.
The image formed at the focus of the objective in an astronomical telescope is ___ and inverted.
The image formed by the objective is within the ___ length of the eyepiece.
The final image formed by the eyepiece in an astronomical telescope is ___.
The final image formed by the eyepiece is virtual, larger than the first image and ___ with respect to the distant object.
The inverted final image in an astronomical telescope does not cause problems when viewing planets, ___, and moons.
The magnifying power of a refracting astronomical telescope is the ratio of the angle $\beta$ subtended by the ___ to the angle $\alpha$ subtended by the object.
For high magnifying power in a refracting astronomical telescope, the objective needs a ___ focal length.
For high magnifying power in a refracting astronomical telescope, the eyepiece needs a ___ focal length.
To obtain sharp images of distant objects, the objective of an astronomical telescope should have a ___ diameter.
A ___ mirror is often used as an objective instead of a large convex lens in astronomical telescopes.
It is difficult to make a perfect ___ lens of large diameter free from defects.
Most telescopes at astronomical observatories are ___ telescopes.
In a reflecting telescope, parallel rays from a distant object are reflected from the concave mirror to fall on a ___ mirror.
The eyepiece in a reflecting telescope acts as a ___ glass.
The astronomical telescope is not convenient for viewing objects on earth because the final image is ___.
To view terrestrial objects, an astronomical telescope must be modified to produce an ___ image.
In a terrestrial telescope, a ___ lens is used as an eyepiece to produce an erect image.
Terrestrial telescopes can be very ___ and inconvenient to use.
___ reflecting prisms are used in binoculars to reduce the length of terrestrial telescopes.
Total reflecting prisms are used in binoculars to ___ the length of the terrestrial telescope.
A ___ is an optical instrument consisting of a pair of telescopes.
Binoculars employ ___ reflecting prisms.
Successive total internal reflections in prisms greatly reduce the ___ of the telescope.
Binoculars are widely used as ___ telescopes.
___ is the branch of physics that studies the nature and properties of light.
Light and optics provide various applications in our daily life such as reflection and ___ of light.
Lasers, spectrometers, and fibre optics are real-world instruments related to ___.
LASER is an acronym for ___ Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation.
A laser produces a narrow beam of intense, monochromatic, ___ light.
Laser light can be in the visible, infrared, or ___ part of the electromagnetic radiation.
A laser produces stimulated emission of ___ from excited atoms or molecules.
Commercial lasers can have power up to ___ kilowatts in a continuous beam.
Special military lasers can have power up to more than a ___.
One type of laser is a ___ laser.
Another type of laser is a ___ laser.
Lasers can also be classified as ___ lasers.
___ lasers are also known as dye lasers.
Semiconductor lasers are also known as laser ___.
The three essential elements of a laser are laser medium, power supply, and ___ cavity.
The power supply in a laser is used for the ___ process.
The ___ medium is the material emitting the laser beam.
The laser medium can be a gas, liquid, crystalline solid or ___ crystal.
The ___ supply delivers energy to the laser medium to excite it to emit light.
Energy for a laser can be supplied as an electric current, a flash lamp or another ___.
The process of supplying energy to excite the laser medium is called ___.
The optical cavity, also known as a ___, concentrates light to stimulate laser emission.
Photons are reflected back and forth in the optical cavity to increase the probability of ___ emission.
Reflection of photons in the optical cavity helps to sustain laser ___.
The optical cavity consists of two highly reflective ___.
One mirror in the optical cavity acts as a perfect ___.
The other mirror in the optical cavity is a ___ reflector, allowing some light to exit as a laser beam.
Two essential processes to initiate a laser action are stimulated emission and ___ inversion.
In stimulated emission, an atom in an excited state interacts with an incident ___.
Photons radiated by stimulated emission have the same frequency, direction, and ___ as the stimulating radiation.
A ___ inversion is needed for stimulated emission to occur.
Population inversion means the population of the upper level is ___ than that of the lower level.
In population inversion, a photon is more likely to stimulate ___ than to be absorbed.
Laser gain is a net increase in the number of ___ with the transition energy.
To sustain laser action, the lower energy level must be ___.
To sustain laser action, the upper energy level must be ___.
The output laser beam is ___.
Laser emission is ___.
Laser light is ___.
A typical laser beam divergence is around a ___.
A modest laser power can produce a ___ intensity.
A 1 mW He-Ne laser beam is much brighter than the ___.
One unique property of a laser beam is ___.
Coherence means that the light waves in a laser beam are in ___ with one another.
Lasers are widely used in ___ due to their coherence.
Lasers are used in medical applications such as ___ surgery.
Lasers are used for destroying ___ stones.
___ curvature corrections are a medical application of lasers.
Lasers are used in cancer diagnosis and ___.
Fibre-optic endoscopy uses lasers to detect ulcers in the ___.
Lasers find application in dentistry and ___ treatments.
In communication, laser light is used in optical ___ communications.
Lasers are used in space communication for ___ and satellites.
___ range finders are used to determine the distance to an object.
High power lasers are used to cut ___ in industries.
High power lasers are used to drill aerosol ___ and orifices.
Laser light is used to collect information from the ___ code on products.
Lasers are used in the semiconductor industries for ___.
Lasers are used in computer ___.
Lasers are used to store and retrieve information in ___ disc (CD).
Fibre optics works on the principle of ___ internal reflection.
Fibre optics transmits information as ___ pulses along a glass or plastic fibre.
A fibre optic cable can contain from a few up to hundreds of ___ fibres.
An optical fibre is composed of two concentric layers, the ___ and cladding.
The ___ surrounds the glass fibre core in an optical fibre.
The cladding is surrounded by a layer called ___ (plastic buffer).
The core and cladding of an optical fibre have different ___ indices.
The refractive index of the core will be slightly ___ than that of cladding.
Light is total internally reflected if it strikes the core to cladding interface at an angle greater than the ___ angle.
The light ray continues ___ down the length of the fibre cable due to total internal reflection.
If the angle of incidence is less than the critical angle, both reflection and ___ take place.
Due to refraction and absorption, the light beam ___ and dies off over a certain distance.
A useful application of fibre optics is in ___.
Endoscopy is a ___ procedure used in medicine.
Endoscopy is used to examine the interior of a hollow organ or ___ of the human body.
___ use optical fibres to observe an image inside the body.
A bundle of optical fibres is inserted into the body to clearly monitor the ___ organs of the body.
A ___ is a scientific instrument used to separate and measure spectral components.
Spectrometers measure ___ components of a physical phenomenon.
Spectrometers are widely used in biology, chemistry, and ___.
An optical spectrometer consists of a light source, collimator, dispersion elements and a ___.
The collimated beam in a spectrometer is dispersed by a ___ element.
Dispersion elements in a spectrometer can be a prism or ___.
The user can rotate the telescope in a spectrometer to determine the angle of dispersion for each ___ line.
Information from a spectrometer is used to determine the wavelength and the ___ make up of the source.
In a spectrophotometer, a dispersion element separates light into ___.
The change in absorption and emission of light intensity with wavelength allows for the ___ of materials.
Spectrophotometers (UV-Vis spectrometer), spectrofluorometers and ___ spectrometers are common optical spectrometers.
___ is the science of measuring visible light in units weighted by human eye sensitivity.
The human eye responds to light with wavelengths between about ___ nm and 700 nm.
The human eye is more sensitive to ___ light of 555 nm.
In photometry, there are ___ main photometric quantities.
The four main photometric quantities are luminous flux, luminous intensity, luminance, and ___.
___ flux (luminous power) $\Phi$ is a measurement of the power of visible light produced by a light source.
Luminous flux is measured in ___ (lm).
One lumen is equivalent to ___ watt of light with a wavelength of 555 nm.
___ intensity $I_v$ is a measure of the luminous flux emitted by a light source in a direction per unit solid angle.
Luminous intensity is measured in ___ (cd) or lumen per steradian.
The ___ is the SI basic unit for luminous intensity.
A ___ angle $\Omega$ is a measure of the amount of the field of view from some particular point.
In the SI system, a solid angle is expressed in ___ (sr).
A steradian is the solid angle subtended at the centre of a unit sphere by a ___ area on its surface.
The solid angle subtended by the whole surface area of a sphere is ___ steradian.
___ $L_v$ is the luminous intensity per unit area of light travelling in a given direction.
The SI unit for luminance is ___ per metre squared (cd m$^{-\text{2}}$).
___ $E_v$ is the total luminous flux incident on a surface per unit area.
The SI unit for illuminance is ___ (lx).
Reading areas in school study halls need ___ working lights.
The minimum average illuminance in school study halls should be ___ lux.
The illuminance $E_v$ at any point on a surface is inversely proportional to the ___ of the distance $r$ between the source and surface.
The principle that illuminance is inversely proportional to the square of the distance is known as the ___ square law of illuminance.
